title: Create a Docker container using Dockerfile and store the image in ECR

tasks.title: Task 1: Sign in to AWS Management Console

steps.text: Click on the Open Console button, and you will get redirected to AWS Console in a new browser tab.

On the AWS sign-in page, Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab. Now copy your User Name and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
On the AWS sign-in page,
Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab.
Now copy your User Name and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Once Signed In to the AWS Management Console, Make the default AWS Region as US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1.

tasks.title: Task 2: Create a Key Pair for the EC2 instances, inside the ECS Cluster

steps.text: Navigate to EC2 by clicking on the Services menu in the top, then click on EC2 in the Compute section
In the left navigation pane (scroll down) within Network & Security, click on the Key Pairs
To create a new key pair, click on the Create Key pair button.
Task 2: Create a Key Pair for the EC2 instances, inside the ECS Cluster
Fill in the details below:
Name: Enter WhizKeyPair
Key pair type: Select RSA
Private key file format: Select pem (Linux & Mac Users) or ppk (Windows users)
Leave other options as default.
Click on the Create Key pair.
Task 2: Create a Key Pair for the EC2 instances, inside the ECS Cluster
Key pair will be created.
Make sure you are in the N.Virginia Region. Navigate to Elastic Container Service by clicking on the Services menu in the top, then click on Elastic Container Service in the Containers section.
On the left sidebar, click on the Clusters option present under the Amazon ECS section, then Click on the Create cluster button.
Task 3: Launching an ECS Cluster
In Cluster configuration :
Cluster name : Leave the name as default.
Task 3: Launching an ECS Cluster

In Infrastructure Section : Choose Fargate and Self-managed instances option Auto Scaling group (ASG) : Select Create a new Auto Scaling group - advancedProvisioning model: On-demand Operating System/Architecture : Select Amazon Linux 2 (Make sure you have selected the mentioned AMI)EC2 instance type : Select t2.microEC2 instance role : Select role name start with Ecs_role_random_numbers

Choose Fargate and Self-managed instances option Auto Scaling group (ASG) : Select Create a new Auto Scaling group - advancedProvisioning model: On-demand Operating System/Architecture : Select Amazon Linux 2 (Make sure you have selected the mentioned AMI)EC2 instance type : Select t2.microEC2 instance role : Select role name start with Ecs_role_random_numbers

EC2 instance role : Select role name start with Ecs_role_random_numbers

Maximum : Enter 2

Task 3: Launching an ECS Cluster
In Network settings:
Security group: Select Create new security group.
Security name : Enter mysg
Security group description : Enter mysg
Inbound rules for security groups:

Task 3: Launching an ECS Cluster
Keep rest things as default and click on the Create button
ECS Cluster will be created
It will take few minutes to provision the ECS Instance.
Task 3: Launching an ECS Cluster

In this task, we are going to create a task definition for the cluster. A task definition provides a blueprint for creating tasks, which are the basic unit of work in ECS. Each task definition can define one or more containers that are run together on the same underlying EC2 instances or Fargate tasks.
On the left sidebar, click on the Task Definitions option present under the Amazon ECS section.
Click on the Create new task definition button.
Task 4: Create Task Definitions
In Task definition configuration:
Task definition family: Enter ecs-demo
Task 4: Create Task Definitions

For Environment: App Environment : Remove AWS Fargate And Select Amazon EC2 Instances. Task Size : CPU : Enter .25 VCPU Memory : Enter .5 GBTask role: select NoneTask Execution role: select None For Container Details : Enter httpd
Image URL: Enter httpd:2.4
For Port mappings: Container Port: Enter 80
Protocol: Select TCP
App protocol: Select HTTP
For Environment:
App Environment : Remove AWS Fargate And Select Amazon EC2 Instances.
Task 4: Create Task Definitions
Task Size : CPU : Enter .25 VCPU Memory : Enter .5 GBTask role: select NoneTask Execution role: select None

tasks.title: Task 5: Create a service and start HTTPD container in ECS

steps.text: In this task, we are going to create a service and start the HTTPD container. In AWS ECS (Elastic Container Service), a service is a long-running task that ensures that a specified number of instances of a task definition are running and maintained in an ECS cluster. Services allow you to define the desired state of your tasks and automatically handle task placement, scaling, and recovery.
On the left sidebar, click on the Task definitions option present under the Amazon ECS section.
Select ecs-demo and click on Deploy and click Create service button.
Task 5: Create a service and start HTTPD container in ECS
Select Task definition revision as 1(latest revision)
Service name: Enter ecs-demo-service
Task 5: Create a service and start HTTPD container in ECS
Existing cluster : Select the created Cluster.
Compute options : Select Launch Type
Launch type: Select EC2
7?. In Deployment configurations :
Service type: Select REPLICA
Desired tasks : Enter 1
Task 5: Create a service and start HTTPD container in ECS
?14. Under Networking section,
Security group: select Use an existing security group
Security group name: select mysg
Task 5: Create a service and start HTTPD container in ECS
Keep other options as default, and click on the Create button, the service will be created within few minutes.

tasks.title: Task 6: Test the HTTPD container in ECS Cluster

steps.text: On the left sidebar, click on the Clusters option present under the Amazon ECS section.
whiz ECS Cluster will be listed here, click on the whiz.
To view the ECS Instance, switch to the Infrastructure tab and scroll down to Container instances
Click on the EC2 instance ID.
Task 6: Test the HTTPD container in ECS Cluster
You will be redirected to see the running EC2 instance.
Task 6: Test the HTTPD container in ECS Cluster

tasks.title: Task 9: Create a repository in ECR and run the push commands

steps.text: Navigate to Elastic Container Registry by clicking on the Services menu at the top, then click on Elastic Container Registry.
Click on Create under Create a Repository.
Fill in the below details to create an ECR repository. Repository name: Enter whiz Keep other options as default.
Fill in the below details to create an ECR repository.
Repository name: Enter whiz
Keep other options as default.
Finally, click on the Create Repository to create an ECR Repository.
The repository is now created, select the repository and click on the View push commands button to upload the created custom image in the ECR Repository.
Task 9: Create a repository in ECR and run the push commands
Copy the commands one by one and paste the same on the terminal to execute.
Since we have already built the Dockerfile to the docker image. We can skip copying and pasting the 2nd command. You can copy the commands according to your System OS.
Task 9: Create a repository in ECR and run the push commands
After running all the 3 commands, can click on the repository name to check the created image.
Task 9: Create a repository in ECR and run the push commands
Image is now pushed to ECR repository, it can now be used in task definition to create services and tasks.

AWS ECS is often used in conjunction with other AWS services, such as Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) to store and manage container images, AWS CloudFormation for infrastructure as code, AWS IAM for access management, and AWS CloudWatch for monitoring and logging.
Once the lab steps are completed, please click on the Validation button on the Right side panel.
This will validate the resources in the AWS account and displays whether you have completed this lab successfully or not.

On the left sidebar, click on the Repositories option present under the Amazon ECR section.
whiz ECS repository will be listed here.
To delete the service, do the following task: Select the present repository, Click on the Delete button.
To delete the service, do the following task:
Select the present repository,
Click on the Delete button.
Task 11: Delete AWS Resources
Confirm the deletion by click on the Delete button.
Task 11: Delete AWS Resources
It will be deleted automatically.
Navigate to Clusters from the left navigation panel. Select the cluster and Click on the Delete Cluster button.
Task 11: Delete AWS Resources
Confirm the deletion by entering the phrase delete whizcluster and click on the Delete button.
Task 11: Delete AWS Resources
Deletion will take up to 3-5 minutes.
You have successfully created and launched Amazon ECS Cluster.
You have successfully created an ECR repository.
You have successfully pushed the custom image to the ECR repository.
Sign out of AWS Account.

Once you have completed the steps, click on End Lab from your lab lab console and wait till the process gets completed.
